items.title: From Trust to Verifiability: Learning Blockchain Through Real-World Constraints
items.contentSnippet:
Have you ever wondered what would happen if someone quietly gained access to the database of a security-critical application? In systems where trust and data integrity matter the most, protection isn’t just about hiding information — it’s about proving that it hasn’t been altered. While working on a recent project that dealt with sensitive civic data, this question stopped feeling theoretical and became something we had to answer in practice.
This question eventually led me to explore blockchain — not as a buzzword, but as a mechanism for proving that data hasn’t been altered. What mattered wasn’t the hype around cryptocurrencies, but a simple architectural promise: data written once cannot be silently changed. Information is recorded in hashed, append-only blocks distributed across multiple nodes, making unnoticed tampering practically impossible. Instead of trusting a database to stay correct, the system allows you to verify the integrity of its history. That shift — from trust to verifiability — was the idea I needed to explore further in my project.

To see whether this idea held up outside theory, I decided to test it in practice during an ongoing hackathon. Our goal was to use blockchain to secure sensitive consumer records and SLA agreements — details I can’t fully disclose — but the broader challenge was clear: could the promise of verifiable integrity actually survive real-world constraints?
The backend was built with Django, chosen primarily for Python’s simplicity and the speed with which it allowed me to prototype a working interface. My initial plan was to use Amazon Managed Blockchain, but persistent network issues forced me to look for alternatives within the limited time available. This constraint led me to explore other providers, eventually settling on a Sepolia-based Ethereum setup integrated through MetaMask. The availability of ready-to-use testnets and the ability to experiment without exposing payment credentials made it the most practical choice under hackathon conditions.
Integration, however, proved to be the most challenging part of the system. Blockchain reads and writes are inherently slow because of cryptographic processing and network confirmation latency, often taking anywhere from a few seconds to over ten seconds to finalize. To handle this, I introduced an asynchronous task queue using Celery with Redis as the broker. This fit naturally within the Python ecosystem and established an architectural foundation that could scale beyond the prototype stage.

Despite this, the performance and cost constraints made it impractical to rely on blockchain as the primary data store. Instead, I maintained a supplementary SQL database for fast application reads, using the blockchain layer primarily for verification and integrity. While this introduced the overhead of synchronizing two sources of truth, it reflected a more realistic balance between trust guarantees and system performance. I briefly considered Redis-based caching, but the volume and persistence requirements made a dedicated database the more appropriate solution.
This was my first time working hands-on with blockchain in a production-like scenario, and there are undoubtedly architectural decisions I would revisit with more time. Still, the process of confronting real constraints — latency, cost, synchronization, and usability — proved far more instructive than theory alone. These experiences now shape how I evaluate architectural trade-offs in every system I approach going forward.

items.title: AI: The Car We Drive, Not the Driver Itself
items.contentSnippet:
AI isn’t here to replace developers — it’s here to challenge how we think, build, and create smarter.
Introduction
I made my portfolio website in the past four days, and somewhere between debugging and deploying, I realized just how massive of a deal AI has become.
You can see how it’s reshaping the developer ecosystem — not in a strictly good or bad way, but in an inevitable one. AI is reducing stress for programmers, accelerating productivity, and redefining what “coding” even means.
It reminded me of how calculators must’ve once revolutionized the world — not by replacing mathematicians, but by amplifying their potential.
The core truth is simple:
AI shouldn’t be treated as a foe for programmers, but it shouldn’t be worshipped either.
I see people completing entire assignments, submissions, and even projects using AI. That’s not necessarily wrong — not every topic needs a deep dive. 
But that doesn’t mean we ignore the things that actually matter.
When AI Stumbled During My Build
While building my website, I had an experience that made this even clearer.
I needed to make an API call to LeetCode to fetch my profile data and display it dynamically. Pretty straightforward. But then, I wanted to go further — to display the activity map for the last six months.
AI helped me implement it. It worked… for a while. Then I got rate-limited for three hours.
That’s when I realized something crucial — if my site couldn’t handle just me as a user during development, what would happen if I scaled it up to hundreds?
AI didn’t have a proper long-term solution. It suggested caching the response in the browser, but that didn’t reduce the number of API calls. It missed the core architectural flaw.
The real solution came when I thought deeper — a unified cache system using Redis. That would reduce load, handle scalability, and keep things smooth for multiple users.
AI couldn’t figure that out — not because it lacked knowledge, but because it lacked critical reasoning. It didn’t understand the crux of the problem.
The Assistant, Not the Coach
That incident solidified one thing for me:
AI is an assistant, not a coach.
It can generate quick fixes, write code, and even offer optimization suggestions. But it doesn’t think long-term. It doesn’t weigh trade-offs, scalability, or system architecture the way humans do.
At the end of the day, we have to understand the architecture, the logic, and the impact of our decisions.
I’ve also noticed many people obsessing over which AI model or platform is the “best for coding.” To me, that’s missing the point entirely. Implementation can be done by any AI — the logic comes from within you.
And then there’s the question of data privacy and ethics. AI isn’t yet equipped to store or process sensitive data securely. It’s still susceptible to manipulation and ethical dilemmas. The more we rely on it blindly, the more vulnerable our systems become.
The Bigger Picture
The funny thing is — the tasks that once felt like nightmares for developers five years ago are now ridiculously simple with AI. That’s not a bad thing.
It means we can now aim higher.
AI gives us leverage — a way to focus on optimization, architecture, and real-world impact rather than repetitive grunt work. Even kids can make websites today, and that’s incredible. But that just means developers should raise the bar — to build things that actually matter.
Let’s use AI not as a shortcut, but as a springboard to create projects with purpose — solutions that drive sustainability, scalability, and creativity.
Conclusion
AI isn’t replacing us — it’s just giving us a faster car to drive.
But remember:
We’re still the ones holding the steering wheel.
So, take control. 
Build smarter. 
And never stop thinking outside the box — because that’s one thing AI still can’t do.

items.title: 217. Contains Duplicate
